1: A shadow: "To you I lift up my eyes, O you who are enthroned in the heavens." The one enthroned in heaven is now identified as Christ, the ascended Lord — Hebrews 1:3 ("sat down at the right hand of the Majesty on high"); Revelation 1:12-18 (the risen Christ enthroned in glory). Eyes lifted to heaven for mercy are eyes lifted to the exalted Christ who intercedes (Romans 8:34).
2: A theme: the servant's eyes fixed on the master, waiting for direction. Christ modeled this perfect attentiveness toward the Father throughout his ministry — John 5:19 ("the Son can do nothing by himself; he can do only what he sees his Father doing"). He is both the pattern of dependence and the mediator through whom the mercy the servants seek is obtained (Hebrews 4:16 — "approach the throne of grace with confidence").
3: A shadow: "Have mercy upon us, for we have had more than enough of contempt." Christ bore the contempt of the world so that God's mercy could flow to those who cry for it. Hebrews 12:2 — he "endured the cross, despising the shame." He is the propitiation that makes this plea answerable (1 John 2:2); the mercy we ask for is the mercy he purchased.
4: A type: the scorn of the proud and the ease of the contemptuous directed at the lowly. This is the reception Christ received at the cross — Matthew 27:39-44 (those who passed by derided him; the chief priests mocked him; the robbers reviled him). The righteous sufferer scorned by the proud is the type of which Christ's passion is the antitype. He bore our shame (Hebrews 12:2) so we could receive honor in him.
